**Mahi-mahi**, also called **dolphinfish** or **dorado**,[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)*[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)*[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3) is a saltwater fish found in warm water.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

When alive, mahi-mahi has flashy gold and green skin,[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4) but this fades to grey after death.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) The head is blunt and rounded,[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) and the body is quite long,[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) generally from 4 to 20 pounds (1.8–9 kg).[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) The skin is thick and can be pulled off by hand.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) When raw, the flesh is pinkish, cooking to off-white.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4) It is lean with a mild flavor and firm, dense texture.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

Mahi-mahi can be purchased whole or filleted.[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) Store like other fish. It freezes well.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)

## Preparation

[edit | edit source]

The skin of mahi-mahi is tough and unpalatable, so it should be removed from the fillet.[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4) If desired, cut off the so-called "blood-line", which is the dark region of flesh running down the fillet.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) It is perfectly edible, but it has a stronger flavor than the rest of the flesh.[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4) Remove any bones if present.[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

The firm texture means it holds up well during grilling or broiling,[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4) though it is at risk of drying out. To prevent this, moist heat cooking techniques or using a sauce/marinade are recommended.[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)

## Substitution

[edit | edit source]

Due to similar properties, halibut and swordfish make reasonable substitutes for mahi-mahi.[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)
